content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To    :  File:  Sections 86109(b)(4) and          Date  : 7/15/83
             87207(a)(1)                            No.:  M-83-177




From  :  FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Jeanne Pritchard

Subject:  Telephone conversation with Kevin Wolf, Friends of the River

         (1)  Friends of the River (FOR) is a lobbyist
employer.  Friends of the River purchased some river raft
trips from a river trip company at "costs" (the river trip
company sells the trips for $960; FOR bought them from the
company for $190). If FOR provides legislators with the
trips, what is the reportable value of the gift received
by the legislators.

     Answer:  The value of the gift is the "fair market
value" — i.e., what it would cost the recipient to purchase
the goods or services on the open market.  The value of the
gift which must be reported by FOR on its Lobbyist Employer
Report, and by the legislators receiving the trips, is $960.

     (2)  Because FOR does not want a legislator to have
to disqualify on a matter which FOR has an interest in, may
FOR return the trips to the company and ask the company to
give the trips to the legislators and thereby avoid a conflict
of interest on the part of the legislators?

     Answer:  No. Because FOR would have control over who
receives the trips, the company would merely be acting as an
intermediary in the making of the gifts, so FOR would still
be the source of the gifts.

     (3)  If FOR suggests to the company that, if it has
any more free trips available in the future, it offer them
to legislators, would FOR be considered the "source" of the
gifts?

     Answer:  No.  FOR may make recommendations regarding
to whom the gifts should be offered, and even to whom the
gifts should be given, without becoming a source.  So long
as FOR does not have any actual control over providing the
trips, they are not a source, and no conflict of interest
would exist for the legislators with regard to FOR.

     (None of these scenarios involves a lobbyist.)

sections.question_synthetic: What is the reportable value of river raft trips provided by Friends of the River to legislators, can FOR avoid legislator conflicts of interest by having a company provide trips instead, and would FOR be considered the source of gifts if it recommends the company offer trips to legislators?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The reportable gift value is fair market value ($960), not the cost paid by FOR ($190). FOR cannot avoid conflicts by using a company as an intermediary if FOR controls the gift distribution. However, FOR may make recommendations about gift recipients without becoming the source, as long as FOR has no actual control over providing the trips.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Memorandum format with three numbered Q&A sections but no formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION headers. Date 7/15/83, No. M-83-177. Contains three distinct advice responses addressing gift valuation, intermediary arrangements, and source attribution under FPPC Political Reform Act sections.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What is the reportable value of river raft trips provided by Friends of the River to legislators, can FOR avoid legislator conflicts of interest by having a company provide trips instead, and would FOR be considered the source of gifts if it recommends the company offer trips to legislators?

CONCLUSION: The reportable gift value is fair market value ($960), not the cost paid by FOR ($190). FOR cannot avoid conflicts by using a company as an intermediary if FOR controls the gift distribution. However, FOR may make recommendations about gift recipients without becoming the source, as long as FOR has no actual control over providing the trips.

embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum addressing three questions regarding gift reporting value and source attribution when Friends of the River (a lobbyist employer) purchases discounted river trips for potential distribution to legislators.
